slides.title: A Different Canada, By Age
slides.statNote: Down from 44.1% in 2011. Source: Statistics Canada (NHS/Census 2011–2021)
slides.body:
For much of the twentieth century, Canada promised each generation that hard work would produce a better life than the one before. Stable employment, accessible housing, and affordable education formed the foundation of what many Canadians saw as a reliable social contract.

For many young Canadians, the basic milestones of adulthood are becoming harder to reach. Renting an apartment, saving for a down payment, building wealth, or paying off school costs now often feels less like a normal stage of life and more like a moving target. The evidence suggests this is not just a feeling. Across housing, wealth, wages, and education, younger Canadians are facing a different economic reality than older generations did at the same age

This generational divide is not simply the result of bad luck or individual choices. It is deeply connected to political decisions about housing policy, taxation, labour markets, and social supports. Understanding the roots of this divide requires examining how policy choices and economic structures have evolved over time.

The most rigorous way to measure this is to compare cohorts at the same life stage—homeownership among 30–34-year-olds in 2011 versus 2021, for instance. When you do, the picture is stark.
slides.question: If the country is richer, why does it feel harder?

slides.title: Housing Split the Generations
slides.statNote: Down from 59.2% in 2011. Source: Statistics Canada (NHS/Census 2011–2021)
slides.body: Housing is at the centre of that divide. The research base describes it as the main

turning price increases into long-term intergenerational gaps, because younger adults have seen homeownership fall at the exact ages when earlier cohorts were most likely to buy homes. Statistics Canada comparisons show that among Canadians aged 25 to 29, the homeownership rate fell from 44.1 percent in 2011 to 36.5 percent in 2021. For those aged 30 to 34, it dropped from 59.2 percent to 52.3 percent over the same period. Meanwhile, rates for older groups remained much higher, with Canadians aged 70 to 74 still around 74.8 percent in 2021.

By contrast, the homeownership rate for Canadians aged 70–74 held nearly constant—75.5%, 75.8%, and 74.8% across those same three Census years.

This is not a lifecycle story. The oldest cohorts stayed put. The youngest fell behind—at the same ages where previous generations had already formed households, purchased homes, and begun accumulating equity.

Several forces converged to produce this outcome. Housing supply in growing urban regions failed to keep pace with demand. Zoning regulations restricted higher-density development. Population growth and immigration increased demand. Low interest rates expanded borrowing capacity. And housing became increasingly treated as an investment asset rather than a place to live.

In cities such as Toronto and Vancouver, housing prices have grown several times faster than wages over the past two decades. Canada's overall homeownership rate was 66.5% in 2021—ranking 23rd among OECD countries, below the OECD average of 71.5%.
slides.question: Did timing become destiny?

slides.title: Rent Took the Slack
slides.statLabel: Renters spending 30%+ of income on shelter
slides.statNote: Compared to 16.1% for owners. Source: Statistics Canada, Canadian Housing Survey 2022
slides.body:
Statistics Canada defines 'unaffordable housing' as spending 30% or more of household income on shelter costs. In 2022, 22.0% of all Canadian households met that definition. But the burden falls unevenly: renters (33.0%) are more than twice as likely as owners (16.1%) to face unaffordable shelter costs—a gap Statistics Canada describes as persistent over time.

The situation is compounded for those entering the rental market now. The Canadian Housing Survey reported that 'new renters' pay substantially higher rents than longer-tenure renters, reflecting the gap between rents locked in under existing leases and rents on newly available units.

Higher rent leaves less room for saving, for schooling, and for starting a family. For many young Canadians, the question is not whether to rent or own—it is how long to rent before any path to ownership becomes conceivable.

First-time homebuyers' median outstanding mortgage rose from $249,350 in 2018 to $280,000 in 2022—a 12% jump in just four years, in a period when incomes did not rise nearly as fast. The financial threshold to enter ownership keeps rising, making each year of renting simultaneously more expensive and less of a savings opportunity.
slides.question: What does adult life cost now?

slides.title: Pay Did Not Keep Up
slides.statNote: Full-time jobs: +24%. Part-time jobs: +6%. Source: Statistics Canada Labour Force Survey
slides.body:
Statistics Canada reports that from 1981 to 2024, median real hourly wages grew 20% overall—roughly 0.5% per year in inflation-adjusted terms. That is modest, but it exists. The more important story is in the distribution.

From 1981 to 1998, wage growth for 25–34-year-olds lagged 45–54-year-olds by roughly 20 percentage points. From 1998 to 2024, 25–34-year-olds partially recovered, with about 5 percentage points faster growth than the older group—but that recovery barely offsets the earlier divergence.

Youth unemployment tells another part of the story. Recent Labour Force Survey releases show youth unemployment spiking in 2025 and remaining elevated into early 2026 relative to other age groups. Job quality matters too: Statistics Canada's 'Quality of Employment' reporting documents long-run trends in involuntary part-time work from 1997 to 2022, showing that the problem is not only job quantity but job stability and earnings reliability.

Even small wage gaps compound over time: essentials rise, paychecks lag, and the savings required to cross the threshold into homeownership keep receding. For workers who entered the labour market after 2000 in major urban centres—precisely the cohort that faced housing price acceleration—the timing has been doubly difficult.
slides.question: Is this a temporary spike—or the new baseline?

slides.title: The Starting Line Moved
slides.body:
Statistics Canada reports average tuition for full-time Canadian undergraduates at $6,834 in 2022/2023, with projections reaching $7,734 for 2025/2026. The trend line in real (inflation-adjusted) terms shows tuition climbing faster than consumer prices across most of the past three decades.

For student debt, official data offers two complementary windows: debt at graduation (tracked every five years through the National Graduates Survey) and the stock of outstanding student loans within household balance sheets (tracked through the Survey of Financial Security). Together they paint a picture of young adults entering the housing market and family formation years already carrying significant financial obligations.

One notable policy shift: the federal government permanently eliminated interest on Canada Student Loans effective April 1, 2023. This reduces the ongoing repayment burden for federally-held debt—but does not change the principal balances, does not address provincial loan portions or private borrowing, and does not reduce the broader affordability constraints facing young adults.

For many, adulthood begins with five figures of debt. Delayed milestones—moving out, family formation, regional mobility—follow naturally. The connection to the housing story is direct: student debt reduces the savings capacity needed for down payments, and the years spent repaying debt are years not spent accumulating equity.
slides.question: What happens when the first step costs more?

slides.title: The Wealth Gap Runs Deep
slides.statNote: Up from $30,000 in 1999, but still far below the 55–64 cohort at $873,400. Source: Statistics Canada Survey of Financial Security, constant 2023 dollars
slides.body:
The Survey of Financial Security provides an unusually strong long-run view of median net worth by age, reported in constant 2023 dollars. In 1999, the median net worth of a household with a major income recipient under 35 was $30,000. By 2023, that had risen to $159,100—a dramatic percentage gain, driven largely by asset price movements including housing.

But the absolute comparison tells a different story. The 55–64 age group held median net worth of $873,400 in 2023, compared to $457,300 in 1999. The 35–44 group went from $161,200 to $409,300. The gap between age groups did not close; in many respects it widened.

Interpretation requires care. Net worth is strongly lifecycle-driven—older households typically have higher wealth because they have had more years to accumulate it. The most rigorous generational claims compare cohorts at the same age across different calendar periods. When you do that for Canadian data, the evidence consistently shows that younger cohorts have faced steeper entry barriers to wealth accumulation than older cohorts did at the same age.

A particularly striking finding from Statistics Canada: people born in the 1990s whose parents were homeowners were about twice as likely to own in 2021 as those whose parents were not homeowners. Parental multiple-property ownership is associated with even higher relative likelihoods. The divide is not only 'young vs old'—it is 'young with family assets vs young without.'
slides.question: Is the wealth ladder being pulled up?

slides.title: Policy Created Feedback Loops
slides.statLabel: CMHC housing supply gap estimate by 2030
slides.statNote: The Parliamentary Budget Officer estimates a lower figure of 1.3 million. Source: CMHC 2022; PBO 2024
slides.body:
The causal picture behind generational inequality in Canada involves several overlapping mechanisms—and competing explanations worth presenting honestly.

The supply-driven frame holds that restoring affordability requires a major increase in housing construction. CMHC's 2022 work projected an additional 3.5 million units by 2030 to restore affordability, with later updates suggesting annual housing starts would need to nearly double—to roughly 430,000 to 480,000 units per year over the next decade. The Parliamentary Budget Officer has explicitly contrasted this figure with its own lower estimate of 1.3 million, attributing the difference to definitions of the affordability target being used.

The demand and finance frame connects monetary policy to housing outcomes. The Bank of Canada publishes a housing affordability index defined as housing-related costs relative to average household disposable income, and its research shows how mortgage rate changes affect both house prices and rents—linking the post-2020 rate cycle directly to household affordability pressures.

The financialization channel focuses on investor ownership. Statistics Canada reports that in downtown census tracts with many condominiums, roughly half of condos were rented by investors in 2020 and 2021. Policy debates increasingly ask whether supply increases alone are sufficient, or whether investor demand creates a floor on prices that new construction cannot overcome.

Once a policy creates winners, those winners gain influence—making the system harder to change. Housing policy sits across federal, provincial, and municipal jurisdictions, often misaligned. If responsibility is shared, accountability diffuses.
slides.question: Can a policy ever truly be reversed?

slides.title: The Promise of Mobility
slides.statLabel: Absolute intergenerational income mobility (Canada, birth cohort 1985)
slides.statNote: Share of adult children whose income at age 30 exceeded their parents' at the same age. Source: Statistics Canada synthesis of mobility research
slides.body:
A Statistics Canada synthesis of recent mobility research defines absolute intergenerational income mobility as the share of adult children whose income is higher than their parents' income at the same age. For adult children born in 1985, the estimated mobility rate at age 30 was 57% in Canada—meaning roughly 4 in 10 Canadians born that year earned less at 30 than their parents had at the same age.

For context: the United States reported 50% for comparable cohorts; the United Kingdom 65%; Norway 74%; Finland 57%. Canada sits in the middle of this range, though the research also flags that relative mobility—the persistence of income rank across generations—may have been declining in earlier work.

The intergenerational transfer channel matters enormously for how inequality compounds. Parental homeownership is strongly associated with young adult homeownership outcomes. Those born in the 1990s whose parents were homeowners were twice as likely to own in 2021 as those whose parents rented. When the primary asset of older generations appreciates rapidly, the wealth transfer to the next generation—through gifts, inheritances, and co-signed mortgages—becomes a significant determinant of who can participate in ownership.

This means the generational divide is not only 'Millennials vs Boomers.' It is 'young people with asset-holding parents vs young people without.' Within-generation inequality can dominate: family wealth, immigration pathway, and region of residence can all outweigh the generation label.
slides.question: Is Canada still a country where origins do not determine outcomes?

quotes.text: I graduated into a world where my parents' advice doesn't apply anymore. They bought their house on a single income. I can't even afford a one-bedroom on two.
Saving for a down payment feels like filling a bathtub with a teaspoon while someone leaves the tap running.
I got in just in time, but I worry about my kids constantly. I don't know how they're going to do it without our help.
The ladder I climbed has had its lower rungs removed. And nobody seems to want to talk about who did the removing.
We didn't realize we were lucky until we saw what happened to the next generation. We just thought we were working hard.
The rules changed, and the young are paying the price. I'm not sure the people who set those rules want them changed back.

ethicsNote: Interview quotes are illustrative of documented patterns in Statistics Canada survey data. Specific attribution and consent procedures apply to published interviews.

policyOptions.title: Accelerate Housing Supply
policyOptions.description: Zoning reform, approvals streamlining, and infrastructure finance to enable the 430,000–480,000 annual starts CMHC estimates are needed.
policyOptions.tradeoff: Long construction lags; local political resistance; regional mismatch if supply appears where demand is weakest.

policyOptions.title: Scale Deeply Affordable Housing
policyOptions.description: Directly fund non-market and social housing to serve renters and low-income households that the private market will not reach.
policyOptions.tradeoff: Requires sustained public funding and delivery capacity; can be slow without existing land and building pipelines.

policyOptions.title: Anti-Financialization Measures
policyOptions.description: Data collection, taxes, and rules on investor activity to reduce 'housing as asset' dynamics alongside supply increases.
policyOptions.tradeoff: Design complexity; may have unintended effects on rental supply if applied bluntly; enforcement and data needs are significant.

policyOptions.title: Income and Wage Supports
policyOptions.description: Strengthen income-side interventions—wage floors, rent assistance, housing benefits—to address affordability directly for renters.
policyOptions.tradeoff: High fiscal cost; debates over targeting effectiveness; wage growth alone may not catch up to asset price appreciation.

policyOptions.title: Reduce Education Cost Burden
policyOptions.description: Extend interest elimination on student loans, control tuition growth, and reduce the debt load young adults carry into adulthood.
policyOptions.tradeoff: Does not solve housing supply; tuition is only one part of total cost of living through post-secondary education.
